Introduction and Review of Probability Theory
Course Goals:
Review a very broad collection of methods so you are introduced to the most fundamental statistical procedures.
Provide you with both theoretical and practical background, including real data examples.
Understand the assumptions and limitations of statistical methods.
Being able to select the appropriate method for analyzing a specific dataset.
What is Statistics?
A branch of mathematics that learns from data
Pause : Then what is machine learning??
Applied statistics: concerned with methods for collecting, analyzing, and drawing conclusions from data.
Theoretical statistics: establishes the properties and conditions under which the data analysis methods work.
Without rudimentary knowledge of how it works, people can't make evaluations of a wide variety of numerical encounters in daily life.
Many statistical methods are relevant and useful in a wide range of academic and non-academic disciplines, beyond just the statistical and data sciences.
Example
Data collected in 19 th century by Darwin who studied the effect of pollination on Zea mays plants (aka maize or corn). See 01-Introduction.R file pot pair type height
Research question:
Do cross-fertilized plants tend to grow taller?
How do we answer this question scientifically?
12 - darwin.maize
You can examine the difference between the two groups visually.
Or you can write a probabilistic model using random variables and then test a statistical hypothesis.
Perform a hypothesis test:

> t.test(d2$diff) One Sample t-test data: d2$diff t = 2.148, df = 14, p-value = 0.0497 alternative hypothesis: true mean is not equal to 0 95 percent confidence interval: 0.003899165 5.229434169 sample estimates: mean of x 2.616667
What do these results mean and how was this test developed?
Key idea in statistics:
explain the variation

To do this we need to express scientifically the variation in the data as observations of random variables and use a statistical model to explain it (at least partially). Statistical analysis requires data, which may be viewed as an observed sample from a population.

The data X 1 , … , Xn are considered as n , usually independent and identically distributed, random variables with a common population distribution/model. This population can be specified as a formula with either its cumulative distribution function (cdf) F ( x ) or its probability density function (pdf) f ( x ).
Back to the maize example
Let a typical height of the self-pollinated plant be denoted Y
Let a typical height of the cross-pollinated plant be denoted X
Then one possible model is:
𝑌 = 𝜇 + 𝜎𝜀 𝑋 = 𝜇 + 𝜂 + 𝜎𝜀
𝜇, 𝜂, and 𝜎 have unknown values called model parameters. They need to be learned from the data.
𝜀 is a random variable, called error or noise
The research question is now: can we demonstrate that 𝜂 > 0 ?
There are always assumptions made to answer the research question. For example, 𝜀 ~ 𝑁(0,1) .
Back to the maize example (continued):
In the context of a sample from a population, X and Y will
have indexes to indicate that.
Also, it is very common to use indexes for the different groups (in this case two groups: self and cross-pollinated).
Then a more rigorously written model would be:
𝑌 𝑖 = 𝜇1 +𝜎𝜀1𝑖, 𝑖 = 1, …, 𝑛 1 𝑋𝑖 = 𝜇2 +𝜎𝜀2𝑖, 𝑖 = 1, …, 𝑛
Research question would be: can we show that 𝜇2 > 𝜇1 ?
There are more details: Darwin controlled the effect of temperature and humidity by planting in the same pot several pairs of cross-pollinated and self-pollinated plants.
This means we must consider such dependence (see R file). 18
Maize example continued:
We can estimate population average 𝜇 with the sample average ത 𝑌
We can estimate population average 𝜇 + 𝜂 with sample average ത 𝑋
Then to answer the research question we intuitively estimate 𝜂 with 20.19167 - 17.575 = 2.616667
So, the estimated typical difference between cross and self-pollinated plants is about 2.6 inches and is positive, but …
How accurate/reliable is this estimate?
Can we safely conclude it is positive not by random chance, but due to systematic difference between the two groups? 19
Some Answers
-To 'prove' there is a systematic difference, we need to formalize the model and then use probability theory results.
-The answer will clearly depend on the sample size n and is usually more reliable as n increases.
-The assumptions play a key role in the trustworthiness of the result. Are the sample observations independent and identically distributed (iid); is the population normally distributed; …
Review of Important Probability Theory Results
Random Sample
The observed sample is a collection of numbers, also known as the observations, the data or the data set. These are represented using lowercase letters, for example, x 1 , … , x n .
The random sample is a collection of random variables whose properties form our model for the data.
Let X 1 , … , Xn be iid random sample from a population with pdf f ( x ).
This means:
Each Xi , i = 1, … , n is a random variable.
Each Xi , i = 1, … , n has the same pdf f ( x ).
X 1 , … , Xn are mutually independent.

Convergence
There are several types of convergence of an infinite sequence { Xn } = X 1 , X 2 , … of random variables to another random variable (or constant) X :
Almost sure (a.s.), also known as strong, which happens when 𝑃 𝑠: 𝑋𝑛 𝑠 → 𝑋 𝑠 = 1
In probability, also known as weak
In distribution: { Xn } converges in distribution to X if P( Xn ≤ x ) → P( X ≤ x ) as n → ∞, for all x
Notations are 𝑋 , 𝑋𝑛 → 𝑋 and 𝑋𝑛 → 𝑋
𝑋𝑛 a.s. 𝑃 𝑑 , respectively.
There is also Lp convergence if 𝐸 𝑋𝑛 - 𝑋 𝑝 → 0
Quadratic Mean Convergence
A special case of also Lp convergence, which is defined as 𝐸 𝑋𝑛 - 𝑋 𝑝 → 0 , is the quadratic mean or L 2 convergence:
𝑋𝑛 → 𝐿2 𝑋 ⟺ 𝐸 𝑋𝑛 - 𝑋 2 → 0
The L 2 convergence is especially relevant in statistics, because it is related to mean squared error (MSE) of an estimator ෠ 𝜃𝑛 :

Statistical Inference and Modeling for Data Science
Define estimators Study properties of estimators
Parametric vs. Nonparametric Models
Our data are X 1 , … , Xn which are iid random sample from a population with a pdf f ( x ) or cdf F ( x ).
Very often the population distribution is not completely known, and f ( x ) depends on an unknown parameter 𝜃 , which can be indicated with f ( x ; 𝜃 ).
When f ( x ) depends on an unknown parameter 𝜃 , we say that we are considering a parametric model for our data.
Example : linear regression is a parametric model.
Non-parametric methods are statistical techniques that do not rely on specific assumptions about the underlying distribution of the population being studied.
Example : K-Nearest Neighbor is a nonparametric model.
In this course we focus on the parametric models.
Goal of Estimation
We assume we know the formula for f ( x ; θ ), except for the value of θ , which is an unknown parameter.
Assume θ is identifiable, that is,
if θ 1 ≠ θ 2, then f (x; θ 1) ≠ f (x; θ 2)
Goal: Use the observed sample values x 1 , x 2 , ... , x n to estimate  .
This means to produce a formula ෠ 𝜃𝑛 = 𝑔(𝑋1, … , 𝑋 𝑛 ) such that theoretically ෠ 𝜃𝑛 ≈ 𝜃

Recall : When we develop theoretical properties, we use X 1 , … , Xn to denote the sample, emphasizing it is a collection of random variables. When we talk about the actual data numbers, we use x 1 , … , x n

Definitions
Definition: Let X 1 , X 2 , ... , Xn be a random sample from f ( x ; θ ). Any formula (aka statistic)
෠ 𝜃𝑛 = 𝑔(𝑋1, …, 𝑋 𝑛)
is called a point estimator of θ .
Definition: The bias of a point estimator ෠ 𝜃𝑛 is defined as
𝐵𝑖𝑎𝑠 ෠ 𝜃𝑛 = 𝐸 ෠ 𝜃𝑛 -𝜃
If 𝐸 ෠ 𝜃𝑛 = 𝜃 , then ෠ 𝜃𝑛 is called unbiased .

Consistency
Definition: If ෠ 𝜃𝑛 ՜ 𝑃 𝜃 as n → ∞, then ෠ 𝜃𝑛 is said to be a consistent estimator of θ .
Example 1 (continued):

(Now we answered the first question.)
Accuracy
We all heard about 'outliers' and how badly they affect the sample average ҧ 𝑥 . But what does this mean? The underlying general issue is how reliable and accurate an estimator መ 𝜃𝑛 is.
That is, how 'close' is መ 𝜃𝑛 to 𝜃 and under what condition it stays close.
How do we even measure the accuracy, given the fact
we don't even know the value of 𝜃 ?
Standard Error
Definition : The standard error of an estimator መ 𝜃𝑛 is defined as Var( መ 𝜃𝑛) and is denoted SE( መ 𝜃𝑛) .
That is,
SE(መ 𝜃𝑛) = Var(መ 𝜃𝑛)
Notes:
The MSE is the risk function with respect to the squared error loss function.
The exact formula for MSE is often too difficult to derive explicitly, so we might have to approximate it.
Mean Squared Error
Definition : The mean squared error (MSE) of an estimator መ 𝜃𝑛 is defined as:
MSE መ 𝜃𝑛 = 𝐸 መ 𝜃𝑛 -𝜃
Theorem 2 :
Mean Squared Error
MSE ෠ 𝜃𝑛 = 𝑉𝑎𝑟 ෠ 𝜃𝑛 + 𝐵𝑖𝑎𝑠(෠ 𝜃𝑛) 2
Note : This result is often referred to as the biasvariance trade off .
Two estimators of the same parameter θ.
෠ 𝜃1 has zero bias but high variance, so it may produce estimates very far from the true value of θ.
By contrast, ෠ 𝜃2 is biased but has much lower variance, so it would typically give More accurate estimates.
Recall:
The expected value is linear: for any r.v. X and Y and constants a and b , we have E( aX + bY ) = a E( X ) + b E( Y )
For the variance, a similar property is
Var (aX + b) = a 2 Var(X)

Unlike the closed-form analytical solution (i.e., the normal equations) available for linear regression, the score function for logistic regression is transcendental. That is, there is no closed-form solution so we must solve with numerical optimization methods such as Newton's method (IRLS) which is done in several iterations.
Although we have focused on simple logistic regression, this likelihood (and score) function generalize directly to the case of p predictors.

Weak Law of Large Numbers
Recall the WLLN:
If 𝑋1, … , 𝑋𝑛 is a random sample from any distribution with a finite mean 𝜇 , then ത 𝑋𝑛 ՜ 𝑃 𝜇 as n → ∞
In many important cases, the sample mean coincides with the MLE of the population mean, so WLLN guarantees that in these cases MLE is consistent!
Q: Is it true in general that any MLE is consistent, even if we are not estimating the mean?
Properties of MLEs
Consistency: If ෠ 𝜃MLE is the MLE of θ , then under some mild regularity conditions ෠ 𝜃MLE ՜ 𝑃 𝜃 as n → ∞ .
This means that in most cases the MLEs are guaranteed to be consistent!
Efficiency: Under the same conditions, the MLEs are asymptotically efficient.
Q: What does this even mean?

Weak Law of Large Numbers
Recall the WLLN:
If 𝑋1, … , 𝑋𝑛 is a random sample from any distribution with a finite mean 𝜇 , then ത 𝑋𝑛 ՜ 𝑃 𝜇 as n → ∞
In many important cases, the sample mean coincides with the MLE of the population mean, so WLLN guarantees that in these cases MLE is consistent!
Q: Is it true in general that any MLE is consistent, even if we are not estimating the mean?
Properties of MLEs
Consistency: If ෠ 𝜃MLE is the MLE of θ , then under some mild regularity conditions ෠ 𝜃MLE ՜ 𝑃 𝜃 as n → ∞ .
This means that in most cases the MLEs are guaranteed to be consistent!
Efficiency: Under the same conditions, the MLEs are asymptotically efficient.

A confidence interval for a parameter 𝜃 is a pair of statistics [ l ( X 1 , … , Xn ), u ( X 1 , … , Xn )] such that

Interpretation
Q : What does 95% confidence mean?
A : The procedure we are using to construct the interval is 95% reliable. That is, 95% of all the intervals constructed with this method will cover the unknown value of 𝜇 , and 5% will miss it.
Therefore, we don't say 𝜇 ∈ ( l , u ) with probability 95%, because 𝜇 is not a random variable! The best way express the interpretation is to say:
We are 95% confident that 𝜇 is between l and u .
100 simulated 95% Cls
Interpretation
Note on the width of the CI
The width of the confidence interval, that is, its accuracy depends on:
The sample size n : the larger the sample size the narrow the width of the CI.
The confidence level: the higher the confidence the wider the CI will be!
The standard deviation of the population or SE: the larger the SE the wider the CI will be.

Lesson Goals
Understand the basic concepts of resampling methods for model selection and assessment.
Understand the similarities and differences between the validation set approach, leave-one-out cross-validation, and K -fold cross-validation.
Understand the basic theoretical concepts of the bootstrap method for assessing statistical accuracy.
Recognize that the performance of machine learning models depends on their prediction capability on independent test data.
Resampling Methods
Involves repeatedly drawing samples from a training set and refitting a model or estimator of interest on each sample to obtain more information about the fitted model/estimator.

Example: We can estimate the variability of a linear regression fit by repeatedly drawing different samples from the training data, fitting a OLS regression to each new sample, and then examining the extent to which the resulting fits differ.
Model Assessment: having chosen a final model, estimating its prediction error on new data.
Model Selection: estimating the performance of different models in order to choose the best one.
Resampling Methods (cont.)
▪ Cross-Validation
-Used to estimate test set prediction error rates associated with a given statistical model, or to select the appropriate level of model complexity.
▪ Bootstrap
-Used mostly to provide a measure of accuracy of a parameter estimate or of a given statistical method.
▪ Test Error
-The average error that results from using a statistical method to predict the response on a new observation.
-The prediction error over an independent test sample.
Training Error
-The average loss over the training sample:

Model Assessment (cont.)
As the model becomes more and more complex, it uses the training data more and can adapt to more complicated underlying structures.
Hence, there is a decrease in bias but an increase in variance.
However, training error is not a good estimate of the test error.
Training error consistently decreases with model complexity.
A model with zero training error is overfit to the training data and will typically generalize poorly.

Model Assessment (cont.)
If we are in a data-rich situation, the best approach for both model selection and model assessment is to randomly divide the dataset into three parts: training set, validation set, and test set.

The training set is used to fit the models. The validation set is used to estimate prediction error for model selection. The test set is used for assessment of the prediction error of the final chosen model.
A typical split might by 50% for training, and 25% each for validation and testing.
Model Assessment (cont.)
Best solution: use a large designated test set, which is often not available. For the methods presented here, there is insufficient data to split it into three parts.
There are some methods to make mathematical adjustments to the training error rate in order to estimate the test error rate:
-Cp statistic, AIC, BIC (we will discuss these next week)

Here, we consider cross-validation (CV) methods that estimate the test error by holding out a subset of the training observations from the fitting process, and then applying statistical method to those held out observations.
For example, leave out 1/10 of subjects, develop model in the remaining 9/10, evaluate in the withheld 1/10, repeat 10 times and average (so called 10-fold CV)
Still not very precise way to measure accuracy

By far, the most important use of validation is for model selection. .. ·
Suppose we have M models; validation can be used to select one of these models.
We use the training data to fit the model, and we evaluate each model on the validation set to obtain the validation errors.
It is now a simple matter to select the model with the lowest validation error.
Overview: Model Selection
Validation Set Approach
Suppose that we would like to find a set of variables that give the lowest validation error rate (an estimate of the test error rate ).
If we have a large data set, we can achieve this goal by randomly splitting the data into separate training and validation data sets.
Then, we use the training data set to build each possible model and select the model that gives the lowest error rate when applied to the validation data set.

Bias -variance tradeoff (again)
· Bias squared:
measures how the predictor is mismatched with the best predictor in expectation
· Variance:
measures how the predictor varies each time with a new training datasets
· Test error rate:
Essentially estimates the MSE (or total) without using formulas.
Problems With a Single Validation Set
Beyond the variability identified in the last plot, another concern is that only a subset of the observations are used in the model training.
In our case, we used 50 instead of 100 observations to fit the model.
Most statistical methods perform more poorly when they are trained on fewer observations, so use of a single validation set may lead to overestimation of the error rate.
Cross-validation is an extension of the validation set approach we just discussed that can address both issues:
Cross-validation averages error rates over multiple samples (i.e., resampling ) to address the variability of MSEs.

Cross-validation fits may be carried out with as many as all but one of the observations in a data set, thereby minimizing the extent to which we overestimate error rate due to lack of sufficient sample size relative to the full sample.
Instead of creating two subsets of comparable size, a single observation is used for the validation set and the remaining observations ( n -1) make up the training set. 123
CV(𝑛) = 1 𝑛 ෍ 𝑖=1 𝑛 MSE𝑖
▪ LOOCV Algorithm:
-Split the entire data set of size n into:
Blue = training data set
Beige = validation data set
-Fit the model using the training data set
-Evaluate the model using validation set and compute the corresponding MSE.
-Repeat this process n times, producing n squared errors. The average of these n squared errors estimates the test MSE.

Validation Set Approach vs. LOOCV
LOOCV has far less bias and, therefore, tends not to overestimate the test error rate.
Performing LOOCV multiple times always yields the same results because there is no randomness in the training/validation set splits.
LOOCV is computationally intensive because the model has to be fit n times. However, there is a shortcut with OLS linear or polynomial regression (where hi is the leverage):

K -Fold Cross-Validation
Probably the simplest and most widely used method for estimating prediction error.
This method directly estimates the average prediction error when the machine learning method is applied to an independent test sample.
Ideally, if we had enough data, we would set aside a validation set (as previously described) and use it to assess the performance of our prediction model.
To finesse the problem, K -fold cross-validation uses part of the available data to fit the model, and a different part to test it.
K -Fold Cross-Validation (cont.)
We use this method because LOOCV is computationally intensive/slow.
We randomly divide the data set of into K folds (typically K = 5 or 10).

One of the folds is treated as a test set, and the method is fit on the remaining K -1 folds. The MSPE is computed on the observations in the held-out fold. The process is repeated K times, taking out a different part each time.
By averaging the K estimates of the test error, we get an estimated validation (test) error rate for new observations.
Tramn
K -Fold Cross-Validation (cont.)
Let the K folds be C1 , … , CK , where Ck denotes the indices of the observations in fold k . There are nk observations in fold k : if N is a multiple of K , then nk = n / K .

Bias-Variance Trade-off for K -Fold Cross-Validation
Which is better, LOOCV or K -fold CV?
-LOOCV is more computationally intensive than K -fold CV
-From the perspective of bias reduction, LOOCV is preferred to K -fold CV (when K < n )
-However, LOOCV has higher variance than K -fold CV (when K < n )
-Thus, we see the bias-variance trade-off between the two resampling methods

We tend to use K -fold CV with K = 5 or K = 10, as these values have been shown empirically to yield test error rate estimates that suffer neither from excessively high bias nor from very high variance
Cross-Validation on Classification Problems

We will cover classification problems in more detail later in the course, but we briefly show how CV can be used when Y is qualitative (categorical) as opposed to quantitative. Here, rather than use MSE to quantify test error, we instead use the number of misclassified observation.

We use CV as follows:
-Divide data into K folds; hold-out one part and fit using the remaining data (compute error rate on hold-out data); repeat K times.
-CV Error Rate: average over the K errors we have computed
The Bootstrap

The bootstrap is a flexible and powerful statistical tool that can be used to quantify uncertainty associated with a given estimator or statistical method; it is a general tool for assessing statistical accuracy.
As an example, the bootstrap can be used to estimate the standard errors of the coefficients from a linear regression fit, or a confidence interval for that coefficient.
The use of the term bootstrap derives from the phrase ' to pull oneself up by one ' s bootstraps. '

The Bootstrap: Overview (cont.)
Suppose we have a model fit to a set of training data. We denote the training set by Z = ( z 1 , z 2 , . . . , z N ) where z i = ( x i , y i ).
The basic idea is to randomly draw datasets with replacement from the training data, each sample the same size as the original training set.
This is done B times, producing B bootstrap datasets. Then we refit the model to each of the bootstrap datasets, and examine the behavior of the fits over the B replications.

S ( Z ) is any quantity computed from the data Z , for example, the prediction at some input point.

From the bootstrap sampling we can estimate any aspect of the distribution of S ( Z ), for example, its variance:
Note that this estimated variance can be thought of as a Monte-Carlo estimate of the variance of S ( Z ) under sampling from the empirical distribution function.

The Bootstrap: An Example
Suppose that we wish to invest a fixed sum of money in two financial assets that yield returns X and Y ; note that X and Y are random variables.
We will invest a fraction α of our money in X , and will invest the remaining 1 -α in Y .
Goal: Since there is variability associated with the returns on these two assets, we wish to choose α to minimize the total risk, or variance, of our investment.

The Bootstrap: An Example (cont.)
We can use this approach for estimating α on a simulated data set, where 100 pairs of returns for the investments X and Y are simulated.
In order to quantify the accuracy of our estimate of α , we also estimate the standard deviation of ො 𝛼 .
To do so, the process of simulating 100 paired observations of X and Y is repeated 1,000 times; now we have 1,000 estimates for α .
The mean over all 1,000 estimates for α →
The standard deviation of the estimates →
The Bootstrap: An Example (cont.)
In rough terms, for a random sample from the population, we would expect ො 𝛼 to differ from α by approximately 0.08, on average.
In the real world, this procedure cannot be applied because for real data we cannot generate new samples from the original population.

The bootstrap approach, however, allows us to use a computer to mimic the process of obtaining new data sets; this enables use to estimate the variability of our estimate without generating additional samples.
Rather than repeatedly obtaining independent data sets from the population, we instead obtain distinct data sets by repeatedly sampling observations from the original data set with replacement .
Each of these bootstrap data sets is created by sampling with replacement , and is the same size as the original data set.
As a result, some observations may appear more than once in a given bootstrap data set and some not at all.
The Bootstrap: An Example (cont.)

Denoting the first bootstrap data set by 𝑍 ∗1 , we can use 𝑍 ∗1 to produce a new bootstrap estimate for α , which we call ො 𝛼 ∗1 .

This procedure is repeated B times for some large value of B (say 100 or 1000), in order to produce B different bootstrap data sets, 𝑍 ∗1 , 𝑍 ∗2 , … , 𝑍 ∗𝐵 , and B corresponding α estimates, ො 𝛼 ∗1 , ො 𝛼 ∗2 , … , ො 𝛼 ∗𝐵 .

The Bootstrap: More Details
Although the bootstrap is used primarily to obtain standard errors of an estimate, it can also provide approximate confidence intervals for a population parameters.
-One approach is known as Bootstrap Percentile confidence interval.
In cross-validation, each of the K validation folds is distinct from the other K -1 folds used for training (i.e. there is no overlap).

To estimate the prediction error using the bootstrap, one approach would be to fit the model in question on a set of bootstrap samples, and then keep track of how well it predicts the original training set.
The Bootstrap: More Details (cont.)

This estimate does not provide a good estimate in general because the bootstrap datasets are acting as the training samples, while the original training set is acting as the test sample, and these two samples have observations in common.
-Each bootstrap sample has significant overlap with the original data.

By mimicking cross-validation, a better bootstrap estimate can be obtained.

Statistical Inference and Modeling for Data Science
Hypothesis Tests
Now that we've studied confidence intervals and point estimators, let's study another commonly used method for statistical inference: hypothesis testing .
Motivating example:
Does gender affect promotions at a bank?
Say you are working at a bank in the 1970s and you are submitting your résumé to apply for a promotion. Will your gender affect your chances of getting promoted?
To answer this question, we'll focus on data from a study published in the Journal of Applied Psychology in 1974.

To begin the study, 48 bank supervisors were asked to assume the role of a hypothetical director of a bank with multiple branches. Every one of the bank supervisors was given a résumé and asked whether the candidate on the résumé was fit to be promoted to a new position in one of their branches.

However, each of these 48 résumés were identical in all respects except one: the name of the applicant at the top of the résumé. Of the supervisors, 24 were randomly given résumés with stereotypically 'male' names, while 24 of the supervisors were randomly given résumés with stereotypically 'female' names.

Comparing these two rates of promotion, it appears that résumés with male names were selected for promotion at a rate 0.875 - 0.583 = 0.292 = 29.2% higher than résumés with female names. This is suggestive of an advantage for résumés with a male name on it.

A hypothesis is a claim about the value of the unknown population parameter.

Definition: The p -value of a hypothesis test is the probability of obtaining a value of the test statistic which is more extreme than the one observed in the current sample, assuming H 0 is true.
More extreme means more contradictory to H 0
Let X be the test statistic (as r.v./formula) and x be its observed value (when calculated from the sample). Then:
If right-tail H a : 𝜃 > 𝜃 0 , then p -value = P( X > x | H 0 )
If left-tail H : < , then p -value = P( X < x | H )
a 𝜃 𝜃 0 0
If two-tail H : 𝜃 ≠ 𝜃 , then p -value = P( |X | > |x | | H )

Example 7:

A movie theater owner likes to keep a very close eye on how much popcorn goes into each bag sold, so he knows that the amount is normally distributed and the average bag has 8 cups of popcorn and that this varies a little bit, about half a cup. That is, the population mean should be μ = 8.00 and the known population standard deviation is σ = 0.50. The owner wants to make sure that the newest employee is filling bags correctly, so over the course of a week he randomly assesses 25 bags filled by the employee to test for a difference ( n = 25). He doesn't want bags overfilled or under filled, so he looks for differences in both directions.

Aside: KL divergence
One way to measure the dissimilarity between two probability distributions, p and q , is known as the Kullback-Leibler divergence (KL divergence) or relative entropy. This is defined as follows:
KL 𝑞 𝑝 = න𝑞 𝑥 log 𝑞 𝑥 𝑝(𝑥) 𝑑𝑥
Typically, q ( x ) represents the 'true' distribution of the data. The pdf p ( x ) then represents a theory, model, description, or approximation of q ( x ), and KL 𝑞 𝑝 measures how close is p to q .
Note: KL is not a proper distance, because it is not symmetric and does not satisfy the triangular inequality.

Permutation Test Example
We use the chickwts data in R. Weights in grams are recorded, for six groups of newly hatched chicks fed different supplements for 6 weeks. There are six types of feed supplements.
The plot suggests that soybean and linseed groups may be similar.
Permutation Test Example
These are the soybean and linseed groups:
The groups can be compared in several ways. For example, sample means difference: 27.67857
or sample medians difference: 27
More generally, one can ask whether the distributions of the two variables differ and compare the groups by any statistic that measures a distance between two samples.
We will use the two-sample t-test as a metric.
Permutation Test Example
If the two samples are drawn from normal populations with equal variances, we can apply the two-sample t-test:

t.test(x, y, var.equal = T) Two Sample t-test data: x and y t = 1.3208, df = 24, p-value = 0.199 alternative hypothesis: true difference in means is not equal to 0 95 percent confidence interval: -15.57282 70.92996 sample estimates: mean of x mean of y 246.4286 218.7500
Permutation Test Example
Assume, however, the distributions of the weights are unknown.
The achieved significance level of the test statistic can be computed from the permutation distribution without requiring distributional assumptions.
If the diets are identical, any split of the combined sample into m = 14 and n = 12 subgroups should result in similar group means.
We will recalculate the value of the test statistic on many different permutations and evaluate how likely the observed t = 1.32 is.
This way, we obtain the so-called empirical p-value .
Permutation Test Example
The approximate permutation test procedure is illustrated below with the two-sample t statistic.

R = 999 # number of replications of the test statistic z = c(x, y) # vectorized data from the two samples K <- 1:26 reps <- numeric(R); t0 <- t.test(x, y, var.equal = T)$statistic #number of replicates #pooled sample #storage for replicates for (i in 1:R) { #generate indices k for the first sample k <- sample(K, size = 14, replace = FALSE) x1 <- z[k] y1 <- z[-k] #complement of x1 reps[i] <- t.test(x1, y1, var.equal = T)$statistic } p <- mean(c(t0, reps) >= t0) > p [1] 0.103
Permutation Test Example
Since we are doing a two-tail test the empirical p-value is 2*(0.103) = 0.206 so the null hypothesis is not rejected. For comparison, the two-sample t-test reports p-value = 0.198.
(The normality tests do not reject the null hypothesis so perhaps the t-test was ok)
Permutation distribution of replicates

Wishart Distribution
The Chi-square random variable with n degrees of freedom is a sum of squares of n independent standard normal variables.
The Wishart distribution is a sum of outer products of normal random vectors .
It is a random matrix which is symmetric and positive definite.
Let X 1 , … , X n ~ N p ( 𝜇 , 𝛴 ). Then the distribution of the p × p random matrix 𝑴=σ𝑖=1 𝑛 𝑿𝑖 𝑿 𝑖 ′ is said to have the Wishart distribution with df = n .

Categorical Predictors

Learn how categorical predictors like sex, region, etc., are incorporated into a regression model
Explain the concept of dummy coding to represent effects of levels of the categorical variables on response
Construct dummy-coded variables for variables with two or more levels and combine with numerical predictors and interaction effects.

Example -Salaries from carData

The 2008-09 nine-month academic salary for faculty in a college in the U.S. The data were collected as part of the on-going effort of the college's administration to monitor salary differences between male and female faculty members. Stored in a data frame with 397 observations on the following 6 variables:
rank : a factor with levels AssocProf AsstProf Prof
discipline : a factor with levels A ('theoretical' departments) or B ('applied' departments).
yrs.since.phd : years since PhD.
yrs.service : years of service.
sex : a factor with levels Female Male
salary : nine-month salary, in dollars.
Source: Fox J. and Weisberg, S. (2019) An R Companion to Applied Regression , Third Edition, Sage.

Categorical from Continuous Predictors
Sometimes you might want to create a categorical predictor from a continuous.
For example, instead of exact amount of income, you may want to split it to just three cases: low-, middleand high-income categories.
Why?
Such model can handle non-linear relationships and provides more flexibility.
These models are known as step-function or regression tree models.
Moving Beyond Lineari ty
The linearity assumption is good in many statistics problems.

However, in some cases, the true relationship between the outcome and a predictor variable might not be linear and as a result the standard linear regression can have significant limitations in terms of predictive power.
There are other methods that offer a lot of flexibility, without losing the ease and interpretability of the linear models:
-Polynomial regression
-Step functions
-Regression and smoothing splines
-Local regression
-Generalized additive models (GAMs)

Tree-Based Methods
Tree-based methods for regression and classification involve stratifying or segmenting the predictor space into several simple regions.
Since the set of splitting rules used to segment the predictor space can be summarized in a tree, these types of machine learning approaches are known as decision tree methods.
The basic idea of these methods is to partition the space and identify some representative centroids.

Regression Trees
In general, we create the partitions by iteratively splitting one at a time the X variables into two regions.
First split at X1 = t 1
If X1 < t 1 , split at X2 = t 2
If X1 > t 1 , split further at X1 = t 3
If X1 > t 3 , split at X2 = t 4

When we create partitions this way, we can always represent them using a tree structure.
As a result, this provides a very simple way to explain the model to a non-expert.

Basis Expansion

Polynomial and piecewise constant regression models are special cases of a basis function approach. Each function f ( x ) within some class (like continuous on a finite interval), can be represented as
𝑓 𝑥 = ෍ 𝑖=1 ∞ 𝑐𝑖 𝑏𝑖 𝑥
where 𝑏𝑖 𝑥 𝑖=1 ∞ is an infinite collection of basis functions.
The idea is to have at hand a family of such functions and approximate the true f ( x ) with only the first K of them.
Instead of fitting a linear model in X , we fit the following model:
𝑦𝑖 = 𝛽0 + 𝛽 1 𝑏1 𝑥𝑖 +𝛽2𝑏2 𝑥𝑖 + …+𝛽𝐾𝑏𝐾 𝑥𝑖 +𝜀𝑖
Note that the basis functions b1 ( . ), … , bK ( . ) are fixed and known, that is, we choose them ahead of time.
Basis Functions
For polynomial regression, the basis functions are 𝑏 𝑗 𝑥𝑖 = 𝑥 𝑖 𝑗
For piecewise constant functions, the basis functions are
𝑏 𝑗 𝑥𝑖 = 𝐼(𝑐 𝑗 ≤ 𝑥𝑖 ≤ 𝑐 𝑗+1)
Other examples are splines and Gaussian basis functions.
This approach relies on math results that show most functions can be approximated with carefully selected basis functions.
Note that we can use OLS to estimate the unknown regression coefficients.
Thus, all the inference tools for linear models (standard errors, F -statistics, etc.) are available in this setting.

Instead of fitting a high-degree polynomial over the entire range of X , piecewise polynomial regression involves fitting separate low-degree polynomials over different regions of X .
Here the beta coefficients differ in different parts of the range of X ; the points where the coefficients change are called knots .

Tests for Normality of Residuals

Shapiro-Wilk Test -involves comparing the observed data with a corresponding set of normally distributed values. The W statistic is derived from the correlation between the data and the corresponding normal scores. A value of W close to 1 suggests that the data follow a normal distribution. The test is known for its good power properties, meaning it has a high probability of correctly detecting departures from normality even in small samples. In R: use shapiro.test
Kolmogorov -Smirnov test -a general test for comparing a sample to any distribution. In R: use ks.test
What if we drop the normality assumption?
There are several alternatives:
Assume another distribution (GLM models)
Transform the data to look 'normal'
Fit a nonparametric regression.
One example of nonparametric model is the socalled Theil's method.
It still assumes the linearity condition, but normality is replaced with
The conditional distribution of Y | x is symmetric and continuous.
The estimation is done by ranking, like the Wilcoxon test.

Linear Model Selection
Ordinary least squares (OLS) regression works well in many real-world applications. Recall, in OLS, we fit a linear model of the form:
𝑌 𝑖 = 𝛽0 +𝛽1𝑥1𝑖 + 𝛽2𝑥2𝑖 + …+𝛽𝑝𝑥𝑝𝑖 + 𝜀𝑖
We find estimates for the betas that minimize the sum of squared errors (SSE):

The solution to the problem can be expressed as:
෡ 𝜷 = 𝑿 ′ 𝑿 -1 𝑿 ′ 𝒀
where the design matrix X is of size n by p +1 because the first column is all ones for the intercept.
Linear Model Selection
In some cases, though, there may be good reasons to use other fitting rules than the normal equations. Why?
-If the sample size n is small and there are a lot of predictors the least squares fit will have high variability.

-If there are a lot of predictors (i.e., p is large), some of them may be non-informative . That is, they may not be related to the outcome variable. In that case, OLS will estimate small coefficients for the variables but will not set the coefficients to be exactly zero.
-Thus, we might seek out a method that sets the magnitude of some coefficients to exactly zero. This would result in feature selection or variable selection .
Model specification is the process of determining which independent variables to include and exclude from a regression equation.
Alternatives to OLS for Linear Models
Subset selection. Instead of fitting OLS on all possible predictor variables, we first eliminate some by setting their coefficients to zero and then fit OLS on the remaining predictors.

Regularization/shrinkage. The model is fit on all p predictors, but the magnitudes of the coefficients are shrunk towards zero. These methods work by adding a penalty to the loss function based on the magnitude of the regression coefficients. One method of regularization, called the lasso , uses SSE plus a regularization term as the loss function:
෍ 𝑖=1 𝑛 𝑌 𝑖 - መ 𝛽0 -෍ 𝑘=1 𝑝 መ 𝛽𝑘𝑥𝑘𝑖 2 +𝜆෍ 𝑗=1 𝑝 መ 𝛽𝑗

the lasso.

Dimension reduction. Here we project the p predictors down into an M -dimensional space, where M < p , via linear combinations of variables, or directions . We then use the smaller set of m projections as the predictors to be fit by OLS.
Partial Least Squares (PLS). A supervised alternative to PCR.

Best Subset Selection
Algorithm for best subset selection:
Let M 0 represent the null model (i.e., the model with no predictors). This model will only estimate a constant intercept which will represent the sample average.
Fit all p choose k models that contain exactly k predictors.
Select the best-fitting model among all the p choose k models, as measured by SSE, R 2 , or some other measure of model fit, and call it Mk .
Pick the single best model from M 0 , … , Mp using cross-validation prediction error, or some other measure of model fit such as the AIC or BIC.

Probabilistic selection: AIC and BIC

The Akaike Information Criterion, or AIC for short, is a method for scoring and selecting a model. It is named for the developer of the method, Hirotugu Akaike, and may be shown to have a basis in information theory and frequentist-based inference. BIC is a similar concept.
The AIC and BIC are both based on -2 times the maximized value of the loglikelihood (-2 LL ); k is the number of parameters and LL is the log-likelihood value at the MLE:
AIC = 2 k / n -2 LL / n, BIC = ln(n)* k -2 LL
Here there is a penalty for magnitude of the LL ; smaller is better
The likelihood (assuming normally distributed errors) for multiple linear regression is
1 2𝜋𝜎 𝑛 𝑒 -1 2𝜎 2 σ 𝑖=1 𝑛 𝑌𝑖 -𝜷 ′ 𝒙𝒊 2
Here there is a penalty for more complexity in the model.
The score, as defined above, is minimized, e.g. the model with the lowest AIC is selected.
AIC and BIC
As you see from their definitions, AIC and BIC are similarly constructed, with the essential difference being the term multiplied by k , the number of estimated coefficients.
As a result (2 vs. log(n)), the BIC tends to penalize model complexity more heavily than the AIC.
The AIC tries to select the model that most adequately describes an unknown, high dimensional reality.
Because the AIC is somewhat more permissive of model complexity than the BIC, it may be preferred for prediction .
When the intent is to create a model for explanation , BIC may be preferred because it will tend to produce a simpler (i.e., more easily interpretable) model.

Importantly, the derivation of BIC under the Bayesian probability framework means that if a selection of candidate models includes a true model for the dataset, then the probability that BIC will select the true model increases with the size of the training dataset. This cannot be said for the AIC score.
Best Subset and Computational Efficiency
Best subset selection is very computationally demanding .
For p = 30, for example, best subset will require fitting 2 30 = 1,073,741,824 models.
Whereas forward selection (described next) will require fitting 1 + 1 + 2 + 3 + … + 29 + 30 = p ( p + 1)/2 + 1 = 451 models.

That said, the ease of fitting Gaussian linear models make even large (i.e., 30+ covariates) problems tractable within a few seconds. This is possible in part due to advances in computational algorithms over the last decades.
The problem of computational efficiency is quickly made worse, however, by using other models that require iterative numerical methods for solving.
For example, with Gaussian linear models (which can be solved analytically), a best subset search with 11 predictors takes less than a second.
By contrast, with a logistic model (solved using Newton's method), 11 predictors takes nearly 2 minutes, and adding each subsequent predictor causes the time to more than double.
Example

Multicollinearity - Variance Inflation Factors
Problems when predictor variables are correlated among themselves
-Regression Coefficients of predictors change, depending on what other predictors are included
-Extra Sums of Squares of predictors change, depending on what other predictors are included
-Standard Errors of Regression Coefficients increase when predictors are highly correlated
-Individual Regression Coefficients are not significant, although the overall model is
-Width of Confidence Intervals for Regression Coefficients increases when predictors are highly correlated
-Point Estimates of Regression Coefficients are wrong sign (+/-)
Multicollinearity -Diagnostics
Large changes in the estimated regression coefficients when a predictor variable is added or deleted.
Nonsignificant results in t-tests on the regression coefficients.
Estimated regression coefficients with an algebraic sign that is the opposite of that expected.
Large coefficients of simple correlation between pairs of predictor variables in the correlation matrix.
Strong linear associations between pairs of predictors in the scatterplot matrix.
Formal diagnostics with Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) for var. X k :
𝑉𝐼𝐹𝑘 = 1 1 - 𝑅 𝑘 2
where 𝑅𝑘 2 is from regressing X k on the other predictors.
If 𝑉𝐼𝐹𝑘 > 10 or 𝑉𝐼𝐹 > 5 , then we have a multicollinearity issue.

Multicollinearity Remedy -Shrinkage Methods

Both the bias and the variance are desired to be low, as large values result in poor predictions from the model. In fact, the model's error can be decomposed into three parts: error resulting from a large variance, error resulting from significant bias, and the remainder - the unexplainable part.
Ridge Regression
Ridge regression is a way to create a parsimonious model when the number of predictor variables exceeds the number of observations, or when a data set has multicollinearity.
The essential idea with ridge regression is identical to OLS regression with one exception, there is a penalty added in the form of the l 2 norm of the coefficients.
The l 2 norm is defined as follows. For x = ( x 1 , x 2 , …, x n ), the l 2 norm of x (also called the Euclidean norm) is
𝒙 2 = ෍ 𝑖=1 𝑛 𝑥 𝑖 2

Statistical Inference and Modeling
Motivating Example: Percent high school graduates
Data source: U.S. Census Bureau
Time period: 2020
Hierarchical Data Structure
Suppose we have data on math scores.
Individual students' scores are nested within schools.
Schools are nested within counties.
Counties are nested within states.
Two levels
Hierarchical Data Structure: Two levels
Suppose we have m groups and data ( X 1 , … , X m ) from each of them, where each 𝑿𝑗 = 𝑋1𝑗 , … , 𝑋 𝑛𝑗 𝑗 .
The random variables, 𝑋1𝑗 , … , 𝑋 𝑛𝑗 𝑗 , nested within Group j should not be independent , because they all belong to the same cluster.
Students within each school perform similarly, because they have the same professors, use same textbooks, etc.
If I know Timmy's math score and know that Tommy is in the same school as Timmy, I already know a little bit about Tommy's score too.
The unique contribution of each student is reduced because some information is shared by knowing which school they are in.
Time to think : Why do we have to account for clustering?
Hierarchical Normal Model
A two-level hierarchical normal model with within group parameters 𝜃 𝑗 , 𝜎 2 and between group parameter 𝜇, 𝜏 2
Within group model:
𝑋1𝑗 , … , 𝑋 𝑛𝑗 𝑗 |𝜃 𝑗 , 𝜎 2 ~ 𝑁 𝜃 𝑗 , 𝜎 2 , 𝑗 = 1, … , 𝑚
Between group model:
𝜃 𝑗 |𝜇, 𝜏 2 ~ 𝑁 𝜇, 𝜏 2
𝜃 𝑗 is the average performance of all students within school j (aka random effects), 𝑗 = 1, … , 𝑚
𝜎 2 measures the within-cluster variability (i.e., at the student level)
𝜇 is the average performance of all students in all schools (aka fixed effect)
𝜏 2 measures the between-cluster variability (i.e., at school level)

Summary: General Hierarchical Data Structure
Two-level hierarchical data model:
𝑋1𝑗, …, 𝑋 𝑛𝑗 𝑗 |𝜙𝑗 ~ 𝑓 𝑥 𝜙𝑗, 𝑗 = 1, …, 𝑚 𝜙1, …, 𝜙𝑚|𝜓 ~ 𝑖𝑖𝑑 𝜋 𝜙 𝜓
How should we represent our information about ?
𝜙1, … , 𝜙𝑚
Best approach: 𝜙1, … , 𝜙𝑚 should be assumed conditionally independent (partial pooling), given a tuning parameter 𝜓 .
The extremes are:
𝜙1, … , 𝜙𝑚 are 'independent' (aka no pooling of information between clusters). Corresponds to 𝜓 → ∞ . This is the approach taken by classical ANOVA models.
𝜙1, … , 𝜙𝑚 are the same (aka complete pooling). Corresponds to 𝜓 = 0 . In this approach we put all observations together and analyze them as if they came from a single cluster.
Hierarchical Data Structure
Key question: how should we represent our information about 𝜙1, … , 𝜙𝑚 ?
The two extremes are:
𝜙1, … , 𝜙𝑚 are independent (aka no pooling of information between clusters). This is the approach taken by classical ANOVA models
𝜙1, … , 𝜙𝑚 are the same (aka complete pooling). In this approach we put all observations together and analyze them as if they came from a single cluster.
𝜙1, … , 𝜙𝑚 are conditionally independent or exchangeable (partial pooling). That is, 𝜙1, … , 𝜙𝑚|𝜓 ~ 𝑖𝑖𝑑 𝜋 𝜙 𝜓
Hierarchical Data Structure
Two-level hierarchical data model:
𝑋1𝑗, …, 𝑋 𝑛𝑗 𝑗 |𝜙𝑗 ~ 𝑓 𝑥 𝜙𝑗, 𝑗 = 1, …, 𝑚 𝜙𝑗|𝜓 ~𝜋 𝜙 𝜓
The first line models the within-group sampling variability.
The second line models the between-group sampling variability.
Notice how the parameters are nested within one another.

Often the parameter 𝜓 will contain some sort of variation component and if it is estimated to be high, this means the clusters were close to independent, and if it is estimated low, this means the clusters were almost identical.
Let the data decide how much info to pool!
Issues in Autocorrelated Data
One of the standard assumptions in the regression model is that the error terms ε i and ε j are uncorrelated for i ≠ j . When error terms are correlated, problems occur when using OLS estimates:
-Regression Coefficients are still unbiased
-MSE underestimates  2
-Standard errors of regression coefficients based on OLS underestimate the true standard error
-Inflated t and F statistics and artificially narrow confidence intervals
Autocorrelated errors of 1 st order:
ε t = ρ ε t -1 + ut
where ut are uncorrelated disturbances assumed normally distributed.
First-Order Autoregressive Model
First-Order Autoregressive AR(1) model for simple linear regression:
𝑌 𝑡 = 𝛽0 +𝛽1𝑋𝑡 + 𝜀𝑡, 𝑡 = 1, …, 𝑛, 𝜀𝑡 = 𝜌𝜀𝑡-1 +𝑢𝑡
The 𝜌 is called autoregressive parameter and we assume that | 𝜌 | < 1 The error terms ut are assumed independent ut ~ N(0, 𝜎 2 ) Same idea generalizes to multiple regression:
𝑌 𝑡 = 𝛽0 +𝛽1𝑋𝑡1 + ⋯+ 𝛽𝑝𝑋𝑡𝑝 + 𝜀𝑡, 𝑡 = 1, …, 𝑛, 𝜀𝑡 = 𝜌𝜀𝑡-1 +𝑢𝑡
It can be shown that under the assumption the errors are stationary:
𝜀𝑡~𝑁 0, 𝜎 2 1 - 𝜌 2, 𝑐𝑜𝑣 𝜀𝑡, 𝜀𝑡-1 = 𝜌𝜎 2 1 - 𝜌 2, 𝑐𝑜𝑟𝑟 𝜀𝑡, 𝜀𝑡-1 = 𝜌
Proof:
𝐸 𝜀𝑡 = 𝜌𝐸 𝜀𝑡-1 +𝐸 𝑢𝑡 = 0 𝑉𝑎𝑟 𝜀𝑡 = 𝑉𝑎𝑟 𝜌𝜀𝑡-1 +𝑢𝑡 = 𝜌 2 𝑉𝑎𝑟 𝜀𝑡-1 +𝜎 2 𝑐𝑜𝑣 𝜀𝑡, 𝜀𝑡-1 = 𝑐𝑜𝑣 𝜌𝜀𝑡-1 +𝑢𝑡,𝜀𝑡-1 = 𝜌𝑉𝑎𝑟 𝜀𝑡-1 +𝑐𝑜𝑣 𝑢𝑡,𝜀𝑡-1
First-Order Model - II

Unequal Error Variances
Here we are discussing the cases 𝑉𝑎𝑟 𝜺 ≠ 𝜎 2 𝑰 𝑛
Possible reasons:
Case 1 -Error Variances known exactly ( very rare case)
Case 2 -Error Variances known up to a constant
-Occasionally information known regarding experimental units regarding the relative magnitude (unusual)
If 'observations' are means of different numbers of units (each with equal variance) at the various X levels, Variance of observation i is  2 / ni where ni is known
Case 3 -Variance (or Standard Deviation) is related to one or more predictors, and relation can be modeled (like Breusch-Pagan Test)
This usually results in biased inconsistent underestimated SEs.
Ordinary Least Squares must be performed with correct variances

Sufficiency attempts to formalize the notion of no loss of information.
A sufficient statistic is supposed to contain by itself all the information about the unknown parameters of the underlying distribution that the entire sample could have provided.
In that sense, there is nothing to lose by restricting attention to just a sufficient statistic in one's inference process.

𝑑 𝑦, 𝜇 = 2 𝑡 𝑦, 𝑦 -𝑡 𝑦,𝜇

Notice that d ( y , μ) = 0 only when y = μ and otherwise, d(y, μ) > 0. In fact, d(y, μ) increases as μ moves away from y in either direction. This shows that d(y, μ) can be interpreted as a type of distance measure between y and μ .

Problems with Linear Regression
Predicted values from linear regression are not constrained to the interval [0,1], even though probabilities should be.
Residuals from the linear model are not normally distributed because they are dichotomous conditional X = x :
𝜀𝑖 = ቊ -𝑝 𝑥 if 𝑌 𝑖 = 0 1 - 𝑝 𝑥 if 𝑌 𝑖 = 1
Also, the variance of ε i is p ( x )[1 -p ( x )], which means it is not constant.
We need a method that deals with the above-mentioned deficiencies.
Logistic Regression: Foundation
Assume the outcome Y is coded as 0/1. Our goal is to specify a model for
𝐸 𝑌 𝑥 = Pr(𝑌 = ȁ 1 𝑋 = 𝑥) = 𝑝 𝑥

Logistic/Sigmoid Function

The sigmoid function resembles an S -shaped curve. It takes the real numbered input values and converts them between 0 to L (by shrinking from both sides, i.e., the negative values to 0 and very high positive ones to L ). Note that it can be decreasing as well.

Example: Bank Marketing
Data in 15-bank-full.csv come from here:
https://archive.ics.uci.edu/dataset/222/bank+marketing

The data are related with direct marketing campaigns of a Portuguese banking institution. The marketing campaigns were based on phone calls. Often, more than one contact to the same client was required, to access if the product (bank term deposit) would be ('yes') or not ('no') subscribed.
Explanatory variable thought possibly to affect the outcome were:
Job occupation: admin. ' , 'bluecollar', 'entrepreneur', 'housemaid', 'management', 'retired', 'selfemployed ' , 'services', 'student', 'technician', 'unemployed', 'unknown',
Housing: has housing loan or no,
Balance: average annual balance
Marital status
The data has 45,211 rows.
Logistic Regression Example
Use ' glm ' function to fit GLM models. 'family' option determines the distribution to be used. Choices are:

binomial(link = "logit") · gaussian(link = "identity") · Gamma(link = "inverse") · inverse.gaussian(link = "1/mu^2") · poisson(link = "log") · quasi(link = "identity", variance = "constant") · quasibinomial(link = "logit") · quasipoisson(link = "log")
See R code
Logistic Regression Example: Some Results

> logit0 = glm(y ~ balance, data = bank, family = "binomial") > summary(logit0) Coefficients: Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|) (Intercept) -2.081e+00 1.595e-02 -130.50 <2e-16 *** balance 3.958e-05 3.840e-06 10.31 <2e-16 ***
Equation: log(odds of subsrcibing) = -2.081 + 0.00003958*balance
Interpretation of the slope:
If the balance increases by 1 euro, then the log of the odds (of subscribing) will increase by 0.00003958 units.
Equivalent (and better!): the odds of subscribing will be multiplied by e 0.00003958 = 1.00004 for each extra 1 euro of balance.
Final interpretation (resale) : For each extra 1000 euros of balance the odds of obtaining the product increase by 4%

Logistic Regression Example:
Dichotomous Covariate
The odds can be interpreted as the relative risk of an event A happening versus not happening. That is,
𝑜𝑑𝑑𝑠(𝐴) = 𝑝𝑟𝑜𝑏(𝐴) 1 - 𝑝𝑟𝑜𝑏(𝐴)

Odds = 0.2 = 1:5 means likelihood of subscribing to the service is 0.2 times the likelihood of not subscribing, within the no housing loan group. Or, you can say that the not subscribing is 5 times more likely than subscribing within the no housing group.
Exercise : Interpret the housing loan = yes group odds.
The odds ratio is the ratio of odds for cases with different x values.
This means that in the absence of a house loan, the odds of subscribing are 2.4 times higher, compared to the group which have a house loan.
Logistic Regression: Estimating Betas
Just as in the simple regression, the coefficients β 0 and β 1 are unknown and must be learned using the available data. Maximum likelihood is usually the method for this problem.

Unlike the closed-form analytical solution (i.e., the normal equations) available for linear regression, the score function for logistic regression is transcendental. That is, there is no closed-form solution so we must solve with numerical optimization methods such as Newton's method (IRLS) which is done in several iterations.
Although we have focused on simple logistic regression, this likelihood (and score) function generalize directly to the case of p predictors.

Logistic Regression: Model Selection
In multiple linear regression there were many selection criteria available: R 2 , R 2 -adjusted, AIC, BIC, …
R 2 is based on the underlying assumption that you are fitting a linear model. If you aren't fitting a linear model, you shouldn't use it!
Only AIC and BIC can be adapted to the GLM with the same formula but now using the logistic likelihood:
AIC = -2log L + 2
BIC = -2log L + log(n)
p p
Rule is the same: smaller AIC or BIC means better model.
Note : regsubsets function from the leaps package is applicable only for linear regression with normal errors. For GLM you must use bestglm function from the bestglm package.

Residuals
Raw residuals e i , aka response residuals are of limited use as each has its own standard error.
In general, when using GLM, it is better to request Pearson residuals , which are equal to the raw residual divided by an estimate of its standard error.
For logistic regression these are
𝑟 𝑖 = 𝑦𝑖 - ො 𝜋𝑖 ො 𝜋𝑖 1 - ො 𝜋𝑖
Note that there are also deviance residuals d i such that 𝐷 = σ 𝑖=1 𝑛 𝑑𝑖 2
For logistic regression,
𝑑𝑖 = 𝑠𝑖𝑔𝑛 𝑒𝑖 -2(𝑦𝑖 log Ƹ 𝑝𝑖 + 1-𝑦𝑖 log(1 𝑝𝑖)

Introduction
Recall: Logistic regression model is applicable when the response variable Y is dichotomous (that is, Y takes only two values).
Now: Response variable Y represents counts (that is, takes only whole integer values).
Summary of Generalized Linear Models (GLM):
Regular linear regression - when Y is continuous numerical.
Logistic regression - when Y is categorical with only two outcomes.
Poisson regression - when Y is a count.
Multinomial or ordinal regression when Y is categorical with more than two outcomes.
Gamma regression -when the outcome variable is positive, like amount of an insurance claim.
Poisson Data Examples
Number of patients in an emergency room at any given time. Possible predictors: time, day of the week, season, …
Number of awards won by a movie. Predictors include number of stars in the movie, budget, IMDB rating, …
Number of children per family, based on income, race/ethnicity, place of residence (urban or rural), …
Assumptions
Observations are independent (standard statistical assumption except in time series analysis and Markov chains)
The distribution of Y (conditional on the X variables) follows a Poisson distribution:
𝑌|𝑿 ~ 𝑃𝑜𝑖𝑠𝑠𝑜𝑛 𝜆 𝑿
Recall that 𝜆 > 0 is a parameter of the distribution equal to both the mean and the variance

Because of the properties of the Poisson distribution, the Poisson regression model is known to satisfy the equidispersion property. If data indicate overdispersion (variance greater than the mean), then alternatives like the negative binomial regression might be more appropriate.
Poisson Regression Model
Log-Linear model:
log[E(Y|X)] = log(𝜆) = b 0 + b 1 X 1 + … + b p Xp or equivalently E(Y|X) = 𝜆 = exp(b 0 + b 1 X 1 + … + b p Xp)

Like the logistic regression, exp( b j ) represents the multiplicative effect of the j th predictor on the mean, keeping the other predictors fixed. That is, increasing Xj by one unit multiplies the mean of the response variable by a factor of exp( b j ).

Empirical evidence shows that with count data the effects of predictors are often multiplicative rather than additive. That is, one typically observes small effects for small counts, and large effects for large counts.

Alternatives
Common violations of Poisson assumptions include
Overdispersion (more variance than predicted by the Poisson)
Zero inflation (too many zeros)
Quasipoisson & negative binomial are usually good options for modeling overdispersion.
In quasipoisson we assume 𝑉𝑎𝑟 = 𝑉𝑎𝑟 𝜇 = 𝜙𝜇
Mixture models can be used to model 'zero inflated' data.

Interpretation of the Poisson Regression parameters
Coefficient for X (the price) = -0.084:

for each extra $1 increase in price, log[E( Y )] decreases by 0.084 units, keeping other predictors fixed. That is, expected quantity sold will multiply by exp(-0.084) = 0.92. This means there is 8% decrease in demand for every $1 increase in price (as per basic economics theory).
Coefficient for D 1 (lowest price in its category), that is -0.912, with exp(-0.912) = 0.4017119:
there is 59.8% decrease in demand if wine is the cheapest in its category and all other predictors are the same.
Which variety is the most popular?
D 3D 7 are all negative, so when any of D 3D 7 are equal to 1, demand decreases, meaning reference region California Chardonnay has the highest demand.
What is the effect on demand if wine is available by the glass?
It increases demand as D 2 has coefficient 1.755
Is the equidispersion assumption satisfied?
Sample mean of Y is 1.38, while sample variance is 7.55, so it looks like assumption is violated. However, we need to adjust for the predictors.

After adjusting for the predictor variables overdispersion drops to 1.148339 (not in base R output, but there is a package for overdispersion test). This still indicates of a problem -should be equal to 1!
An alternative estimate is σ 𝑖=1 𝑛 𝑟 𝑖 2 𝑛-𝑝-1 , where r i are the Pearson residuals (see next slide).

More Complicated GLMs
We studied Gaussian, logistic and Poisson GLMs.
Many datasets include dependent variables whose distributions cannot be represented by the normal, binomial or Poisson distributions.
For example, distributions of common survey questions such as 'How much do you like this class? (1 -7)'.
Sometimes numerical variables, like age and years of education are binned into ordered categories, the number of which may vary from survey to survey.
In such situations, sometimes the research interest lies not in the scale of each individual variable, but rather in the associations between the variables.
Even if the variables are not artificially binned, it is natural to think of many ordinal, non-numeric variables as arising from some underlying latent numeric process.
For example, the severity of a disease might be described 'low', 'moderate' or 'high', although we imagine a patient's actual condition lies within a continuum.
There are specialized, complex distribution for ordered categories, ranks or nominal data.
Ordinal Regression
Ordinal logistic regression is a statistical analysis method that can be used to model the relationship between an ordinal response variable and one or more explanatory variables.
The model is:
log 𝑃 𝑌 ≤ 𝑗 1 - 𝑃 𝑌 ≤ 𝑗 = 𝛽𝑗0 -𝛽1𝑥1 - 𝛽2𝑥2 -⋯-𝛽𝑝𝑥𝑝,𝑗 = 1,…,𝐽 - 1

The model incorporates a negative sign so that there is a direct correspondence between the slope and the ranking. Thus, a positive coefficient indicates that as the value of the explanatory variable increases, the likelihood of a higher-ranking increases.
Ordinal Regression Example

Suppose we are interested in describing the relationship between the educational attainment and number of children of individuals in a population. Additionally, we might suspect that an individual's educational attainment may be influenced by their parent's education level. The 1994 General Social Survey provides data on variables DEG, CHILD and PDEG for a sample of individuals in the United States, where DEG i indicates the highest degree obtained by individual i , CHILD i is their number of children and PDEG i is the binary indicator of whether either parent of i obtained a college degree.
Data:
Smoking and survival for women in Whikcham near Newcastle upon Tyne in northern England. (Appleton et. al. 1996)
Data collected in 1972-1974 and then follow up 20 years later.
Research question:
How does smoking affect survival rate?

Introduction
Last time: Poisson model in which Y is a count.
Count models are fundamentally about rates
Rate of heads per coin toss
Bottles of wine sold per week
Can also estimate rates by modeling time-to-event
Tricky, because cannot ignore censored cases
Left-censored: Don't know when time started
Right-censored: Something cut observation off before event occurred
Ignoring censored cases leads to inferential error
Survival Analysis
Survival analysis concerns a special kind of outcome variable: the time until an event occurs .
Survival Analysis
Survival analysis concerns a special kind of outcome variable: the time until an event occurs .
For example, suppose that we have conducted a five-year medical study, in which patients have been treated for some disease.
We would like to fit a model to predict patient survival time, using features such as baseline health measurements or type of treatment.
Survival Analysis
Survival analysis concerns a special kind of outcome variable: the time until an event occurs .
For example, suppose that we have conducted a five-year medical study, in which patients have been treated for cancer.
We would like to fit a model to predict patient survival time, using features such as baseline health measurements or type of treatment.
Sounds like a regression problem . But there is an important complication: some of the patients have survived until the end of the study. Such a patient's survival time is said to be censored .
Survival Analysis
Survival analysis concerns a special kind of outcome variable: the time until an event occurs .
For example, suppose that we have conducted a five-year medical study, in which patients have been treated for cancer.
We would like to fit a model to predict patient survival time, using features such as baseline health measurements or type of treatment.
Sounds like a regression problem . But there is an important complication: some of the patients have survived until the end of the study. Such a patient's survival time is said to be censored .
We do not want to discard this subset of surviving patients, since the fact that they survived at least five years amounts to valuable information.
Non-medical Examples
Non-medical Examples
CHURN
The applications of survival analysis extend far beyond medicine. For example, consider a company that wishes to model churn , the event when customers cancel subscription to a service.
Non-medical Examples
The applications of survival analysis extend far beyond medicine. For example, consider a company that wishes to model churn , the event when customers cancel subscription to a service.
The company might collect data on customers over some time period, in order to predict each customer's time to cancellation.
However, presumably not all customers will have cancelled their subscription by the end of this period; for such customers, the time to cancellation is censored.
Non-medical Examples
The applications of survival analysis extend far beyond medicine. For example, consider a company that wishes to model churn , the event when customers cancel subscription to a service.
The company might collect data on customers over some time period, in order to predict each customer's time to cancellation.
However, presumably not all customers will have cancelled their subscription by the end of this time period; for such customers, the time to cancellation is censored.
Survival analysis is a very well-studied topic within statistics. However, it has received relatively little attention in the machine learning community.
Survival and Censoring Times
Survival and Censoring Times
For each individual, we suppose that there is a true failure or event time T , as well as a true censoring time C .
Survival and Censoring Times
For everyone, we suppose that there is a true failure or event time T , as well as a true censoring time C .
The survival time represents the time at which the event of interest occurs (such as death).
By contrast, the censoring is the time at which censoring occurs. For example, the time at which the patient drops out of the study, or the study ends.
Survival and Censoring Times -Continued
Survival and Censoring Times -Continued
We observe either the survival time T or else the censoring time C . Specifically, we observe the random variable
Y = min(T, C).
Survival and Censoring Times -Continued
We observe either the survival time T or else the censoring time C . Specifically, we observe the random variable
Y = min(T, C).

If the event occurs before censoring (i.e. T < C ) then we observe the true survival time T ; if censoring occurs before the event ( T > C ) then we observe the censoring time. We also observe a status indicator,
𝛿 = ቐ 1 if T ≤ C 0 if T > C
Finally, in our dataset we observe n pairs ( Y, δ ), which we denote as ( y 1 , δ 1) , . . . , ( yn, δ n ).
Illustration
Here is an illustration of censored survival data. For patients 1 and 3, the event was observed. Patient 2 was alive when the study ended. Patient 4 dropped out of the study.

A Closer Look at Censoring
A Closer Look at Censoring
Suppose that a few patients drop out of a cancer study early because they are very sick.
A Closer Look at Censoring
Suppose that a number of patients drop out of a cancer study early because they are very sick.
An analysis that does not take into consideration the reason why the patients dropped out will likely overestimate the true average survival time.

Similarly, suppose that males who are very sick are more likely to drop out of the study than females who are very sick. Then a comparison of male and female survival times may wrongly suggest that males survive longer than females.
A Closer Look at Censoring
Suppose that a number of patients drop out of a cancer study early because they are very sick.
An analysis that does not take into consideration the reason why the patients dropped out will likely overestimate the true average survival time.

Similarly, suppose that males who are very sick are more likely to drop out of the study than females who are very sick. Then a comparison of male and female survival times may wrongly suggest that males survive longer than females.
In general, we need to assume that, conditional on the features, the event time T is independent of the censoring time C . The two examples above violate the assumption of independent censoring.

The Survival Curve
The survival function (or curve) is defined as
S (t) = Pr(T > t).
This decreasing function quantifies the probability of surviving past time t .
For example, suppose that a company is interested in modeling customer churn. Let T represent the time that a customer cancels a subscription to the company's service.
The Survival Curve
The survival function (or curve) is defined as
S (t) = Pr(T > t).
This decreasing function quantifies the probability of surviving past time t .
For example, suppose that a company is interested in modeling customer churn. Let T represent the time that a customer cancels a subscription to the company's service.
Then S ( t ) represents the probability that a customer cancels later than time t . The larger the value of S ( t ), the less likely that the customer will cancel before time t .

Estimating the Survival Curve
Type III
Young classify species into three distinct categories based

Ecologists classify species into three distinct categories based on their probability of survival. The key differences between these three patterns are related to the stage at which the probability of survival is greatest. Type I species have high probability of survival throughout life with a rapid decline late in life while Type II species are the opposite with a rapid decline early in life and a high probability of survival late in life.
Estimating the Survival Curve
Consider the BrainCancer dataset, which contains the survival times for patients with primary brain tumors undergoing treatment with stereotactic radiation methods.

The predictors are gtv (gross tumor volume, in cubic centimeters); sex (male or female); diagnosis (meningioma, LG glioma, HG glioma, or other); loc (the tumor location: either infratentorial or supratentorial); ki (Karnofsky index); and stereo (stereotactic method).
Estimating the Survival Curve
Consider the BrainCancer dataset, which contains the survival times for patients with primary brain tumors undergoing treatment with stereotactic radiation methods.

The predictors are gtv (gross tumor volume, in cubic centimeters); sex (male or female); diagnosis (meningioma, LG glioma, HG glioma, or other); loc (the tumor location: either infratentorial or supratentorial); ki (Karnofsky index); and stereo (stereotactic method).
Only 53 of the 88 patients were still alive at the end of the study.
Estimating the Survival Curve -Continued
Estimating the Survival Curve -Continued
Suppose we'd like to estimate S (20) = Pr( T > 20), the probability that a patient survives for at least 20 months,
Estimating the Survival Curve -Continued
Suppose we'd like to estimate S (20) = Pr( T > 20), the probability that a patient survives for more than 20 months,
It is tempting to simply compute the proportion of patients who are known to have survived past 20 months, that is, the proportion of patients for whom Y > 20.
This turns out to be 48 / 88, or approximately 55%.

However, this does not seem quite right: 17 of the 40 patients who did not survive to 20 months were censored, and this analysis implicitly assumes they died before 20 months. Hence it is probably an underestimate.
Estimating the Survival Curve -Continued

Alternatively, to estimate S (20), we could consider computing the proportion of patients for whom Y > 20, out of the 71 patients who were not censored by time t = 20; this comes out to 48/71, or approximately 68%.

However, this is not quite right either, since it amounts to completely ignoring the patients who were censored before time t = 20, even though the time at which they are censored is potentially informative. For instance, a patient who was censored at time t = 19.9 likely would have survived past t = 20 had he or she not been censored.

For k = 1, . . . , K, we let r k denote the number of patients alive and in the study just before d k ; these are the at-risk patients.

Nathan Mantel William Haenszel

Females seem to fare a little better up to about 50 months, but then the two curves level off to about 50%. How can we carry out a formal test of equality of the two survival curves?
At first glance, a two-sample t -test seems like an obvious choice: but the presence of censoring again creates a complication.
The Log-Rank Test -Continued
Females seem to fare a little better up to about 50 months, but then the two curves both level off to about 50%. How can we carry out a formal test of equality of the two survival curves?
At first glance, a two-sample t -test seems like an obvious choice: but the presence of censoring again creates a complication.
To overcome this challenge, we will conduct a log-rank test.

The Log-Rank Test -Continued
Recall that d 1 < d 2 < · < dK are the unique death times among the non-censored patients, rk is the number of patients at risk at time dk , and qk is the number of patients who died at time dk .
We further define r 1 k and r 2 k to be the number of patients in groups 1 and 2, respectively, who are at risk at time dk .
Similarly, we define q 1 k and q 2 k to be the number of patients in groups 1 and 2, respectively, who died at time dk . Note that r 1 k + r 2 k = rk and q 1 k + q 2 k = qk .

At each death time dk , we construct a 2 × 2 table of counts of the form shown above.
Note that if the death times are unique (i.e. no two individuals die at the same time), then one of q 1 k and q 2 k equals one, and the other equals zero.

Application to the Brain Cancer Dataset

Application to the Brain Cancer Dataset
Comparing the survival times of females and males on the BrainCancer data gives a log-rank test statistic of W = 1 . 2, which corresponds to a two-sided p -value of 0 . 2.
Thus, we cannot reject the null hypothesis of no difference in survival curves between females and males.
Application to the Brain Cancer Dataset
Comparing the survival times of females and males on the BrainCancer data gives a log-rank test statistic of W = 1 . 2, which corresponds to a two-sided p -value of 0 . 2.
Thus, we cannot reject the null hypothesis of no difference in survival curves between females and males.
The log-rank test is closely related to Cox's proportional hazards model, which we discuss next.

Regression Models with a Survival Response
We now consider the task of fitting a regression model to survival data.

We wish to predict the true survival time T . Since the observed quantity Y = min( T, C ) is positive and may have a long right tail, we might be tempted to fit a linear regression of log( Y ) on X . But censoring again creates a problem .
To overcome this difficulty, we instead make use of a sequential construction, similar to the idea used for the Kaplan-Meier survival curve.

Cox's Proportional Hazards Model

The proportional hazards assumption states that: ℎ 𝑡 𝑥𝑖 = ℎ0 𝑡 𝑒 σ 𝑗=1 𝑝 𝑥𝑖𝑗 𝛽 𝑗 where h 0( t ) ≥ 0 is an unspecified function, known as the baseline hazard . It is the hazard function for an individual with features xi 1 = · = xip = 0.

The name proportional hazards arises from the fact that the hazard function for an individual with feature vector xi is some unknown function h 0( t ) times the factor 𝑒 σ 𝑗=1 𝑝 𝑥𝑖𝑗 𝛽 𝑗 . The quantity 𝑒 σ 𝑗=1 𝑝 𝑥𝑖𝑗 𝛽 𝑗 is called the relative risk for the feature vector xi = ( xi 1 , . . . , xip ), relative to that for the feature vector xi = (0 , . . . , 0).
Proportional Hazards Model -Continued
What does it mean that the baseline hazard function h 0( t ) is unspecified?
Proportional Hazards Model -Continued
What does it mean that the baseline hazard function h 0( t ) is unspecified?
Basically, we make no assumptions about its functional form . We allow the instantaneous probability of death at time t , given that one has survived at least until time t , to take any form.
Proportional Hazards Model -Continued
What does it mean that the baseline hazard function h 0( t ) is unspecified?
Basically, we make no assumptions about its functional form . We allow the instantaneous probability of death at time t , given that one has survived at least until time t , to take any form.
This means that the hazard function is very flexible and can model a wide range of relationships between the covariates and survival time.
Proportional Hazards Model -Continued
What does it mean that the baseline hazard function h 0( t ) is unspecified?
Basically, we make no assumptions about its functional form . We allow the instantaneous probability of death at time t , given that one has survived at least until time t , to take any form.
This means that the hazard function is very flexible and can model a wide range of relationships between the covariates and survival time.
Our only assumption is that a one-unit increase in xij corresponds to an increase in h ( t | xi ) by a factor of exp( β j ).
An Example

Here is an example with p = 1 and a binary covariate xi ∈ { 0 , 1 } . Top row: the log hazard and the survival function under the model are shown (green for xi = 0 and black for xi = 1). Because of the proportional hazards assumption, the log hazard functions differ by a constant, and the survival functions do not cross.
Bottom row: the proportional hazards assumption does not hold.

Partial Likelihood -Computation
Partial Likelihood -Computation
To estimate β , we simply maximize the partial likelihood with respect to β . As is the case for logistic regression, no closed-form solution is available, and so iterative algorithms are required.
Partial Likelihood -Computation
To estimate β , we simply maximize the partial likelihood with respect to β . As is the case for logistic regression, no closed-form solution is available, and so iterative algorithms are required.
In addition to estimating β , we can also obtain other model outputs, like those in least squares regression and logistic regression.
For example, we can obtain p-values corresponding to particular null hypotheses (e.g. H 0 : β j = 0), as well as estimated standard errors and confidence intervals associated with the coefficients.
